1: bĕṣēʾt yiśrāʾēl mimmimṣrāyim bêt yaʿăqōb mēʿam lōʿēz — "when Israel went out from Egypt, the house of Jacob from a people of lōʿēz" — lōʿēz (from lāʿag, to mock, stammer, speak unintelligibly) describes Egypt as a place of foreign/incomprehensible speech, a domain linguistically alien to Israel. The choice of this word marks Egypt as beyond the covenant world. The LXX renders it barbarophōnou (barbarous-speaking people), the etymological source of the Greek word "barbarian."
2: hāyĕtāh yĕhûdāh lĕqodšô yiśrāʾēl mamšĕlôtāyw — "Judah became his sanctuary, Israel his dominion/kingdom" — the verb hāyĕtāh (became, feminine — the country is meant, not the tribe) marks transformation: the land was not always holy but became so through election. The divine name does not appear in this psalm until v7 — everything happens as nature reacting to an unnamed force, building dramatic suspense.
3: hayyām rāʾāh wayyānōs haywardēn yissōb lĕʾāḥôr — "the sea saw and fled; the Jordan turned back/reversed" — the Red Sea crossing (Exod 14) and Jordan crossing (Josh 3-4) are compressed into one dramatic moment. The verb yānûs (to flee, run away) — the sea is terrified and runs; yissōb lĕʾāḥôr (to turn back to the rear) — the Jordan's flow is reversed. No Moses, no Joshua is named — only creation's immediate response to divine presence.
4: hehārîm rāqĕdû kĕʾêlîm gĕbāʿôt kĕbnê-ṣōʾn — "the mountains leaped/danced like rams, the hills like young lambs" — rāqad (to skip, leap, dance; the same verb in Ecclesiastes 3:4 for dancing) applied to mountains achieves the psalm's signature comic-majestic effect. The simile domesticates the mountains into a flock responding to their shepherd — and sets up the interrogation of vv5-6.
5: mah-lĕkā hayyām kî tānûs haywardēn tissōb lĕʾāḥôr — "what happened to you, O sea, that you fled? O Jordan, that you turned back?" — the psalm now speaks directly to the sea and the Jordan as if they could answer. The rhetorical questions (vv5-6) probe the cause of vv3-4, building dramatic suspense before the answer in v7.
6: hehārîm tirqĕdû kĕʾêlîm gĕbāʿôt kĕbnê-ṣōʾn — "you mountains, why did you leap like rams? You hills, like young lambs?" — the same simile as v4, now turned interrogative. The repetition creates a chiastic structure: vv3-4 describe → vv5-6 interrogate → v7 answers. No answer is given to the sea or the mountains; the answer comes as a command to the earth itself.
7: mippĕnê ʾādôn ḥûlî ʾāreṣ mippĕnê ʾĕlôah yaʿăqōb — "from before the Lord (ʾādôn), writhe in anguish O earth; from before the God of Jacob" — the divine name appears for the first time in v7. ḥûl (to writhe, to be in anguish) is the verb used for a woman in labor (Isaiah 26:17; Jeremiah 22:23); the earth's response to YHWH's presence is birth-pangs, not merely trembling. The title ʾĕlôah yaʿăqōb (God of Jacob) emphasizes covenant particularity: the universal Lord is specifically Israel's covenant God.
8: hahōpekhî haṣṣûr ʾăgam-māyim ḥallāmîš lĕmaʿyĕnô-māyim — "the one who turns the rock into a pool of water, the flint into a spring of water" — a participial relative clause describing God's characteristic action. The reference is to the water-from-rock miracle at Meribah and Massah (Exodus 17:1-7; Numbers 20:2-13). Paul allegorically identifies this rock with Christ in 1 Corinthians 10:4 ("the spiritual rock that followed them, and the rock was Christ"). ḥallāmîš (flint, the hardest stone) heightens the miracle: not ordinary rock but flint becomes a flowing spring.
